title: The K-Shaped Economy Crisis (KEC) Will Persist Unless There Is Structural Regime Change at the Fed

roughly the same level as the Fed funds
rate. And rarely in the history is this
dynamic sustained. So just look at the
dark blue line and the red line in this
chart. Dark blue line being the Fed
funds rate. The red line is the 10ear
nominal Treasury yield. I'm just going
to draw a line over the 10-year nominal
Treasury yield. Stare at the blue line
while I'm doing this. You see how the
the yellow line that I'm drawing is
rarely rarely at the same level of the
blue line. Rarely at the same level of
the blue line. rarely very rarely is the
10-year nominal treasury yield at the
same level as the Fed funds rate. So
what does this mean? Historically the
10ear tenure is roughly on a median
basis 120 basis points wider than the
Fed funds rate. So the current you know
basically flat spread eight basis point
spread implies the 10ear should either
be greater than 5% or hypothesize based
on the U-shaped economy that the Fed
funds rate should be 3%. So, one of
these markets is wrong based on the
historical relationship between the Fed
funds rate and the 10-year nominal
Treasury yield. So, I'm betting that the
market is better at setting rates than
the cabal of people, human beings, this,
you know, 12 person body of human beings
determining the price of money and
quantity of money for the world economy.
I'm guessing that the global financial
markets are better at pricing those
things than this cabal of human beings,
very flawed human beings in many
respects in terms of their inability to
set credible, thoughtful, sound monetary
policy. So, uh, in our view, the Fed
funds rate should already be at 3% based
on, you know, the spread, the historical
spread. The Fed funds rate is at 3%,
then we would have a normal level of
spread between the 10-year and the Fed
funds rate. So, I'm betting on the
market here. Uh historically the
two-year is about 30 basis points wider
than the Fed funds rate. The current
spread of minus 43 basis points implies
the two-year should either be you know
much higher at 4.25%.

might be better at setting the price and

rate should be three or three and a

So we talked about uh how the SLF
essentially will allow the Fed uh to
warehouse bills on its balance sheet but
obviously lend them back out uh you know
at high quantities. So so essentially
this is you know the issue with uh the
Treasury issuing a lot of bills is is is
is great is great because bills are the
most pristine form of collateral in the
repo market. But a lot of times when
there's ever any stress or concern or
nervousness in the financial sector the
repo market starts to get very
fragmented as people start hoarding cash
and hoarding bills and that's not good.
So what the Fed is essentially trying to
do is say, "Hey, let me hoard them all
because I'm happy to lend them out to
anybody." And so that's exactly what's
happening here with the securities
lending facility and obviously the
standing repo facility is a self form of
debt monetization. If if if
people who buy coupon debt from the
treasury, which is pretty much all of
us, private non-bank sector, commercial
bank sector, foreign uh sector, foreign
uh official sector, you know, just
anyone who buys treasuries or at least
the dealers now at least have access to
this facility. they can now swap their
coupons. They can liquify their coupons
for cash. And so what it ultimately
means is that they no longer have to
hoard cash or bills at any point in time
because they know they can always get
cash with the coupons they have. And
it's I mean it's again it's it's liquid.
It's a it's definitely a step in the
right direction because what it's doing
is it's going to reduce the cost and
increase the quantity of credit
available for the real economy via the
repo market. uh you know again it's you
know these facilities aren't huge but we
over time they're going to in our view
they think they're going to get uh huge
uh if we uh continue to see a further
shift a further shift a further uh uh an
acceleration of the dovishnet financing
policy uh that my uh fellow Yelli uh
Scott Bessent has inherited from our
fellow Yelli Janet Yellen. We think
we're going to see a lot more of that
over the medium term as in line with
what we outlined in our recent macro
scouting reports. um you know there's
just a wall of money coming in terms of
u the uh the stable coin issuance that
we're likely to see. So if they want to
be financially repressed, go financially
repress them. The Fed's failure to
acknowledge structurally higher
inflation was not the only reason we've
long called for regime change at the
institution. Its failure to acknowledge
fiscal dominance is also a key factor.

dollars you know in terms of um you know

so this the gap between these two
numbers represents money that is not
going to the housing market it's money
that's not going to small businesses.

medium income consumers.
So, there's a vacuum here of money that
used to go to those sectors uh uh the
economy and it's and it doesn't exist.

to figure out how to make money cheaper
and more available and the Fed hasn't
been doing that. And that's why one of
the many reasons why we have this
growing K-shaped economy. uh the
dramatic increase in the quantity and
cost of federal borrowing is
contributing to the historic crowding
out of consumers and businesses on the
bottom of the K. So as I mentioned we
got 11 trillion dollars of marketable
treasury uh debt supply over the next 12
months another incremental $59 billion
in interest payments and again if you
look at that statistic this 11 trillion
statistic it's 209% of US uh uh savings
um you know in terms of you know US 20%
of US savings obviously which comes from
the private sector household and
corporate sector that's almost double
what it used to be so again as I
mentioned the gap between these two
numbers the long run mean and the
current value represents money that is
not going to the housing market it's
money that's not going to small
businesses. It's money that's not going
to load to medium- income consumers. So,
there's only enough money left to go to
capitalize the AI infrastructure.
There's only enough money left to go to
Elon Musk and Alex Carp and Don and Eric
Jr. or Eric and Don Jr. So, this is why
again, this is stuff. This is part of
the reason. So, I'll end on this. The
frozen housing market is a classic
example of the Fed's too tight monetary
policy, crowding out businesses,
household, and sectors of the real read,
not digital economy. So if you look at
the spread 286 basis points spread
between the effect the marginal mortgage
rate of around 6.5% and the effective
mortgage rate just north of 11 or 4%. Uh
so you know the spread is essentially
creating this lockend effect and the
reason the spread is so wide is because
there's not enough money beating it
down. There needs to be money beating
the red line down. There's no money to
beat the red line down and the spread's
going to be high. And so as long as that
spreads high, then the people who have
the 4% mortgages aren't going to move
because they they're going to have to,
you know, their mortgage payment is
going to increase by two 300 basis
points. And the people who uh don't have
a house yet, but want a house yet, can't
afford it because the marginal mortgage
rates too high. It's a it's a double
whammy. And so this is why the existing
home sales market has been structurally
depressed for three years now. We've
been tracking at uh financial crisis
type lows in existing home sales for the
last three years without a financial
crisis. This is it. I mean, so this is
just a one classic example of crowding
out, you know, crowding. And again, it's
not the Fed's fault that the government
is too big. It's not. I'm not blaming
the Fed for the US, the fiscal policy,
but what I am doing is blaming the Fed
for not responding to fiscal policy
dynamics. Fiscal dominance demands
substantial monetary debasement and
financial repression. If you don't have
monetary debasement and financial
repression, then you're going to get a
crowding out. Period. and the crowding
out equals the bottom of the K getting
bigger and bigger and bigger and so it
seems like the administration's on it
and in our opinion that's a positive for
society and it's a positive for asset
markets. So we'll wrap it up there.
Derry Stale here presenting our round
the horn for Saturday, November 1st,
2025. Best of luck out there. We'll
catch you back here uh next week.
Cheers.
